coredata.dc:title: White-emissive boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for detection of water in organic solvents and Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yuan
Wang, Haiyang
Nie, Tingting
Lu, Yutong
Zheng, Peng
Zheng, Liang
Zhang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method. The synthesis involved citric acid as the carbon source, boric acid as the boron source, urea, and ammonia as the nitrogen sources, with N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) serving as the solvent. The BN-CDs exhibited dual emission peaks at 440 nm and 570 nm under 310 nm excitation wavelength, and white-light emission with chromatographic coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) was achieved. In addition, fluorescence probes based on BN-CDs were carried out in sensing water content in solvent and metal ion. Notably, BN-CDs exhibited a special solvation effect, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.075–1.5 % as fluorescence probes on water content in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), isopropanol (IPA), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) and DMF. Furthermore, both emissions quenched linearly by Fe3+ ions, proposing a sensitive method for selective ion detection with promising detection limits of 4.5 μM. These findings underscore the multifunctional potential of BN-CDs as versatile fluorescent probes for various sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BN-CDs
Heteroatom doping
Detection of water content
Detection of metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of water content in solvents
Detection of water content in organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and applications of CDs.
(a) TEM image and (inset) size distribution and (b) HRTEM images of BN-CDs.
(a) Raman and (b) FTIR spectra of BN-CDs.
(a)Full spectrum of XPS and high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) B1s of BN-CDs.

(a) emission spectra of BN-CDs in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths, (b) excitation spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs, and (c) chromatographic coordinates of BN-CDs (inset: photograph of BN-CDs under 310 nm excitation).

(a) PL and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs in different solvents, (c) fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs in DMSO with different water contents; (d) linear curves of the ratios of green emission to blue emission intensity and the ratios of yellow emission to blue emission intensity as a function of water content.

(a) fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs with different metal ions (1 mM), (b) selectivity study of BN-CDs with different metal ions at 443 and 570 nm wavelength, (c) PL response of BN-CDs in presence of Fe3+, (d) linear relationship between F/F0 and Fe3+ concentration.

head.ce:title: White-emissive boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for detection of water in organic solvents and Fe3+ ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method. The synthesis involved citric acid as the carbon source, boric acid as the boron source, urea, and ammonia as the nitrogen sources, with N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) serving as the solvent. The BN-CDs exhibited dual emission peaks at 440 nm and 570 nm under 310 nm excitation wavelength, and white-light emission with chromatographic coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) was achieved. In addition, fluorescence probes based on BN-CDs were carried out in sensing water content in solvent and metal ion. Notably, BN-CDs exhibited a special solvation effect, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.075–1.5 % as fluorescence probes on water content in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), isopropanol (IPA), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) and DMF. Furthermore, both emissions quenched linearly by Fe3+ ions, proposing a sensitive method for selective ion detection with promising detection limits of 4.5 μM. These findings underscore the multifunctional potential of BN-CDs as versatile fluorescent probes for various sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with carbon as the backbone structure discovered in recent years. They are sphere-like nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm [1]. CDs exhibit the advantages of excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, and easy availability [2,3]. Because of the excellent properties, CDs are widely used in fluorescent sensors [4,5], photocatalysis [6], biomedicine imaging [7,8] and optoelectronic devices [9,10].

In order to improve the performance of CDs, it is a common strategy by doping various heteroatoms to adjust the electronic structure of CDs. Heteroatom doping, such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorus, and so on, shows tremendous potential in tailoring the properties of CDs [11,12]. In addition to single heteroatom doping, dual-doping is also investigated as the synergistic effect between the doped heteroatoms. Generally, CDs doped with nitrogen were chosen to co-doped with other dopants as the introduction of N brings in rich edges and large surface area, which can provide edge active sites. This may alter the chemical structure and surface functional groups of CDs, resulting in improved optical, electrical, and chemical properties.

Boron and nitrogen, are the neighbours of carbon in the periodic table, can be homogeneously doped into CDs for the similar radius. Kim et al. prepared high photoluminescence (PL) boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) via the microwave pyrolysis of citric acid (CA), boric acid (BA) and ethylenediamine [13]. The quantum yield (QY) of BN-CDs were enhanced to 80.8 % by additionally doping boron, which introduces more nitrogen atoms to accommodate the charge density of the carbon matrix. Additionally, it was reported that the synergistic effect of the B and N can impart multiple emission properties to CDs [14–17]. Xue’s group designed BN-CDs with blue and green emissions through a micro-solvothermal method from L-dopa and BA [15]. Compared to blue-emitted N-CDs, the B and N co-doping results in a double-emission channel that allows more electrons to return to the valence band by radiative leap. Deng et al. synthesized red/purple dual-emissive BN-CDs with the absolute fluorescence QY of 86.58 % using 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and o-phenylenediamine [18]. What’s more, B and N heteroatom doping may favor the formation of CDs with solid-state fluorescence (SSF). Yang et al. achieved BN-CDs powder with dual emission by a one-step microwave method [19]. The high-efficiency SSF of NB-CDs was demonstrated to related to the hydrogen bond between B–OH on the surface of CDs and the high content of graphitic N in CDs.

B and N co-doping tunes the optical properties of CDs and holds great promise in the realm of fluorescence detection. Wang’s group developed a novel probe for sensing sialic acids (SA) and neuraminidase (NEU) based on BN-CDs [20]. The phenylboronic acid groups on the surface of BN-CDs can selectively react with SA reducing of fluorescence emission from BN-CDs, and the NEU-catalyzed hydrolysis of SA leads to fluorescence recovery. Yu et al. applied B and N co-doped silicon-CDs (BN-SiCDs) on the detection of dopamine with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.58 ng‧mL−1 [21]. The fluorescence enhancement of BN-SiCDs may be caused by the hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation between BN-SiCDs and DA oligomers. Shi et al. designed BN-CDs modified hydrogel for both visual sensing and extraction of Ag+ ions [22]. The hydrogel exhibits an linear sensing range of 0–300 μM with a LOD of 51 nM and a Ag+ extraction performance of over 251.4 mg‧g−1. Despite the unclear exact mechanisms, it is reasonable to assume that the co-doping of B and N can expand the applications range of CDs.

In this paper, white-emitted BN-CDs were prepared with a one-step solvent-thermal method using citric acid, boric acid, urea, ammonia, in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). Characterization of the BN-CDs was explored by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM), Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman characterization. The synthesized dual-emission BN-CDs were applied on ratiometric fluorescence detection in terms of water content in solvent and metal ions. BN-CDs exhibit a unique solvatochromic effect in organic solvents, which enable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of water content in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), isopropanol (IPA), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Additionally, Fe3+ induces notable quenching in the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs, facilitating efficient and sensitive fluorescence detection of Fe3+.

Boric acid, citric acid, urea, ammonia, DMF, DMSO, orthophosphoric acid, acetic acid, EtOH, Methanol, IPA, Formamid, Ethylene Glycol (EG), NMP, Acetone, Tetrahydrofuran (THF), Acetonitrile, were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and other salts including Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ were obtained from Gaojing Chemical Co. Ltd (Hangzhou, China). All the reagents and solvents were of the analytical grade and used without further purification.

0.5 g boric acid, 5.3 g urea and 3.5 g citric acid were dissolved in 30 mL of DMF solution and stirred well, and then 1.8 mL ammonia were added to the mixed solution. The solution was transferred into in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 160℃ for 6 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained product was centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 30 min to collect the supernatant. Afterwards, the obtained samples were stored at 4℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in solvents
ce:section.ce:para: At room temperature, BN-CDs was added to a solvent mixture of DMSO and water with a gradient of water content from 0 % to 100 % (v/v). These mixed solutions were left to stand for 5 minutes to ensure thorough mixing, and then the PL spectroscopy was carried out under the excitation of 310 nm. The DMSO solvent was substituted with EtOH, IPA, NMP, and DMF, and the same experiments steps were conducted.

In order to verify the selectivity of BN-CDs to Fe3+, selective experiments were carried out. Common metal ions Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ were selected and prepared with deionized water. All metal ion concentrations were consistent (1 mM). 3ul BN-CDs solution was taken, diluted with 3 mL BR buffer with pH value of 5.0, and then a certain amount of metal ion solution was added.

A series of Fe3+ solutions of different concentrations (0 ∼ 1 mM) were prepared, and then a certain amount of BN-CDs solution was added to the ionic solutions, diluted to 3 mL with a buffer of pH 5.0, and the fluorescence spectra were tested at 310 nm after full mixing and reaction for 3 min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) was employed to investigate the microstructural morphologies. XPS analysis utilized a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer, employing Al Ka monochromatized radiation as an X-ray source, with a binding energy of 284.8 eV serving as an internal reference for the C 1 s level. FTIR spectra were acquired using a Nicolet iS20 FTIR Spectrometer. Raman scattering spectra were assessed with a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectroscope with a laser emitting at 514.5 nm wavelength and a power output of 10 mW. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was measured by a Shimadzu 3600 UV–vis near-infrared spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectro fluorophotometer, and the lifetime spectrum was obtained using an FM-4P-TCSPC spectrofluorimeter.

In this work, BN-CDs were prepared by solvothermal reaction in DMF with citric acid, boric acid, urea, and ammonia as precursors ( Fig. 1). The distribution and morphology of the synthesized BN-CDs were characterized by TEM in Fig. 2(a). The BN-CDs are spherical or quasi-spherical and uniformly dispersed. The inset shows the size distribution of BN-CDs, and the average particle size of BN-CDs is about 3.0 ± 0.8 nm. As shown in high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of Fig. 2(b), the lattice spacing of BN-CDs is about 0.21 nm, which generally corresponds to (100) lattices facet of graphitic carbon [23].

Raman spectrum demonstrates the intrinsic characteristics of sp 2 carbon with disorder. Common features were found in the region of 1200–1800 cm−1, where the distinctive peak at 1350 and 1586 cm−1 corresponds to the D and G peaks ( Fig. 3(a)). The G band originates from the graphitized sp2 carbon, and the D band denotes the presence of disordered structures or defects in the carbon dots. The high intensity ratio of the D and G band (∼1.0) indicates the existence of structural distortion caused by the doping of N atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone [24]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), FTIR spectroscopy reveals the composition and chemical structure of the BN-CDs. The broad band in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 correspond to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, and the band detected at 2930 cm−1 can be ascribed to C-H stretching vibrations [25]. The stretching vibration peak of CN and CO is evident at 1645 cm−1 [26]. The peaks at 1387 and 1092 cm−1 arise from B-O [27] and C-B stretching vibrations [28,29], respectively. The peak located at 1256 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of C-O-C [30]. These identified functional groups signify the doping of boron and nitrogen elements during the synthesis of carbon dots.

To gain further insights into the structure and elemental composition of BN-CDs, characterization analyses were conducted using XPS. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the XPS broad-scan spectra of BN-CDs exhibited obvious peaks at 191.7, 284.6, 400.2, and 531.5 eV, corresponding to the peaks of B 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. This observation validates the successful preparation of boron and nitrogen co-doped fluorescent carbon dots. The XPS results indicate that the BN-CDs primarily comprise carbon (71.75 %), nitrogen (8.94 %), oxygen (17.9 %) and boron (1.41 %). The XPS spectra of BN-CDs were further analyzed with results depicted in Fig. 4(b)-(d). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum reveals the signals of 283.8, 284.8, 286.7, and 288.9 eV, attributing to the presence of C-B, C-C/CC bonds, C-O/C-N, and CO functional groups, respectively [31,32]. The XPS spectrum of N 1 s can be divided into two peaks, including C-N (399.8 eV) and N-H (401.4 eV) moieties [32]. Additionally, three peaks with binding energies of 191.4 eV, 192.8 eV and 194.3 eV in the B1s spectrum are mainly assigned to B-C, B-N and B-O [19,33]. The results of FTIR characterization are basically consistent with that of XPS, indicating the doping of the elements of N and B in carbon dots.

The optical properties of BN-CDs were investigated through PL and UV–vis absorption spectra. Fig. 5(a) presents the absorption and fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs. The BN-CDs exhibit three absorption bands at 275, 338, and 400–580 nm. The first absorption band at 275 nm is attributed to the π→π* intrinsic transition, and the second absorption band centered at 338 nm is attributed to the n→π* intrinsic transition [34]. The third broad absorption band distributed in the range of 400–580 nm may be related to the abundance groups such as CO, CN on BN-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs at different excitation wavelengths were depicted in Fig. 5(b), revealing that the emission spectra of BN-CDs are excitation-dependent. The BN-CDs show double emission, with an emission peak near 440 nm, and a band around 520–570 nm region. Notably, under single wavelength excitation at 310 nm, the BN-CDs display two prominent emission peaks at 440 nm and 570 nm, and emit pure white emission with chromatographic coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) (Fig. 5(c)). The excitation spectra of the blue-emitting component at 440 nm exhibit a distinct peak at 350 nm, close to the second absorption band, which may be attributed to the transition of the n→π* eigenstate. The yellow emission component (570 nm) demonstrates an excitation band in a range of 400–560 nm, overlapping with the third absorption band and possibly linked to the “surface band” of CDs.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetimes of BN-CDs were characterized in Fig. S1 and Table S1. The fluorescence decay of BN-CDs at 440 nm and 570 nm was observed under 310 nm excitation. The decay curves of the blue and yellow emissions can be well fitted by a double exponential function. The average lifetimes of the blue fluorescence component of BN-CDs are calculated to be 9.3 ns, and that of the yellow fluorescence component is 11.0 ns, indicating that the blue/yellow luminescence is related to different electronic transition processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of solvents on the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs was investigated by uniformly dispersing in eight organic solvents. The PL spectra of BN-CDs at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6(a). It is noteworthy that the fluorescence of BN-CDs can be roughly categorized into two groups: one group exhibiting a triple-emission phenomenon in solvents such as DMSO, MeOH, EtOH, EG, and IPA, with a green emission band appearing near 525 nm in addition to the blue and yellow emission observed in water. Whereas in another set of solvents such as DMF, NMP and formamide, the yellow emission peak of BN-CDs almost disappeared, while a distinct green emission peak emerged. Furthermore, the blue emission intensities in non-aqueous solvents were consistently lower than those in water. Similarly, the absorption peak at 350 nm undergoes a red shift and absorbance decrease in organic solvents compared to that in water (Fig. 6(b)). Besides, the absorption spectra in most solvents exhibit a similar spectrum, while a new absorption peak emerges near 400 nm in DMF, DMSO, and NMP. According to previous reports, it was hypothesized that the difference in the luminescence phenomenon of BN-CDs in different solvents may be due to the interaction of the carbon dots with the hydrogen bonding between the solvent and water. When the BN-CDs are dispersed in solvents with varying polarity, the density distribution of the electron cloud on their surfaces is altered, leading to solvent-induced chromogenicity.

Organic solvents are widely used in various fields such as petroleum chemical industries, pharmaceutical industries, fuel production, and academic research. Despite water being a universal solvent crucial for human activities, the presence of water in organic solvents has always been a significant issue. Even trace amounts of water in organic solvents can disrupt reactions, reduce product quality and yield. Precisely detecting the trace amounts of water in organic solvents holds great importance. Due to the unique optical properties of multi-emission and solvatochromism effect, BN-CDs offer potential for water detection in organic solvents. When dispersed in DMSO, the original blue emission peaks of BN-CDs in aqueous solution vanished, and the intensity of yellow emission decreased significantly, while a newly generated green emission band emerged near 525 nm. In view of this interesting phenomenon, the effect of different water contents (v/v) in DMSO solvent on the optical properties of BN-CDs was investigated in depth. Fig. 6(c) demonstrated that the blue emission intensity gradually increased with the increase in water content, while the green and yellow emission intensity gradually weakened. The UV–vis absorption peak around 350 nm was found to blue-shifted with rising water content, accompanied by an overall intensity increase, as shown in Fig. S2. Meanwhile, the absorption peak around 550 nm also shifted towards the blue end of the spectrum while the intensity gradually decreases. In addition, the absorption peak at 400 nm diminishes. According to previous reports, the interaction between BN-CDs and solvent is related to hydrogen bond. The XPS results above reveal the presence of pyridine N within the BN-CDs structure, which is surrounded by heightened electron density serving as a hydrogen bond acceptor. It increases the possibility of nonradiative transitions of BN-CDs in the excited state in the presence of hydrogen bond donor molecules like water, thereby resulting in reduced emission of surface states including green and yellow fluorescence.

As shown in Fig. 6(d), the ratio of green to blue emission intensity (Igreen/Iblue) exhibits a good linear relationship with water content in the range of 30 %-100 %. The linear regression equations are Igreen/Iblue= -0.009[H2O]+ 1.650 (R2 = 0.990). Besides, the ratio of yellow to blue fluorescence emission (Iyellow/Iblue) also demonstrates a good linear relationship with water content ranging from 20 % to 100 %, described by a linear regression equation of Iyellow/Iblue= -0.012[H2O]+ 2.218 (R2 = 0.998). The LOD for water content was calculated to be 0.075 % with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3). The excellent linear correlation offers an efficient way for detecting water content in DMSO in the chemical industry. Furthermore, BN-CDs based fluorescent probe are capable of detecting water in EtOH (Fig. S3), IPA (Fig. S4), NMP (Fig. S5) and DMF (Fig. S6) solvents in. As summarized in Table S2, the corresponding LOD was 1.5 %, 0.37 %, 0.45 % and 1.3 %, respectively. Moreover, the BN-CDs have great potential for water detection in organic solvents, as summarized in the comparative study in Table S3.

Iron is one of the most common metallic elements in the environment and it plays essential roles in the human body, such as cell metabolism, enzyme catalysis, electron transfer, oxidation reactions, and synthesis of DNA and RNA. Both the insufficient and excess of Fe3+ can cause various diseases, thus it is necessary to develop green fluorescent probes with highly selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ ion.

In order to investigate the effect of metal ions on the dual-emission of BN-CDs, PL spectra were recorded under 310 nm excitation. Solutions containing different metal ions, such as Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ were introduced to BN-CDs in BR buffer (pH=5.0) at a concentration of 1 mM. As shown in Fig. 7(a) and (b), BN-CDs exhibited a notable response in both emission peaks in the presence of Fe3+ ions. According to references, Fe3+ ions tend to coordinate with surface groups on N-dopped CDs, and contribute to the nonradiative electron transfer process, causing fluorescence quenching [35,36].

As the high selectivity of Fe3+ ion, BN-CDs were applied on the fluorescence detection of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the dual emissions of BN-CDs were gradually quenched with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. It was found that the emission ratio F/F0 at 443 and 530 nm both exhibited a good linear relationship in different Fe3+ concentration ranges of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7(d)). Here F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs in the absence of ions (labeled as 'Blank'), and F represented the fluorescence intensities of BN-CDs in the presence of various metal ions. The linear regression equations were F443/F0 = -0.522[Fe3+]+ 0.701 (R2 = 0.994, 0.4–1.0 mM), F530/F0 = -1.229[Fe3+]+ 0.967 (R2 = 0.980, 0–0.4 mM), and F530/F0 = -0.493[Fe3+]+ 0.675 (R2 = 0.995, 0.5–1.0 mM). Based on S/N = 3, the LOD of 4.5 μM was obtained. Compared to the previously reported fluorescence probes based on CDs for Fe3+ as summarized in Table S4, the linear range is among the best reported to date.

In summary, blue and yellow double-emissive BN-CDs were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method, utilizing boric acid, urea, ammonia, and citric acid as precursors. The resulting BN-CDs emit pure white light with chromatographic coordinates of (0.31,0.33). Furthermore, fluorescence probing experiments demonstrated the versatile sensing capabilities of BN-CDs in response to water content in organic solvent and metal ions. Notably, BN-CDs showed solvatochromism effect led to distinct luminescence changes in different solvents. It facilitates highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensing of water content in organic solvents, exhibiting promising detection limits and linear ranges. In addition, the selective quenching of Fe3+ ions on BN-CD fluorescence was facilitated to propose a rapid and sensitive detection for Fe3+. Overall, this work highlights the multifunctional potential of BN-CDs as versatile fluorescent probes, promising applications in various fields such as solvent analysis and metal ion detection, paving the way for future advancements in optical sensing technologies.

Nie Tingting: Writing – original draft, Validation. Lu Yutong: Visualization. Jiang Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wang Haiyang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Zheng Liang: Resources. Zhang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zheng Peng: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Wenzhou Basic Project of Social Development s2023019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2025.111992.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, Y.P., Zhou, B., Lin, Y., Wang, W., Fernando, K.A. S., et al., Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 128 (2006) 7756-7757. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja062677d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: mysterious past, vibrant present, and expansive future
Trends Chem.

Coord. Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials for fluorescent sensing and imaging
Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Chem. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, structure, properties, and catalytic applications for organic synthesis
Catalysts
